fuzzy_prd:
hey team, we need a library that can read those bank export files (the ones banks give you when you download your statement) and turn them into something developers can actually work with programmatically. right now every dev on the team is writing their own string hacking to get account numbers and transaction lists out of these files and it keeps breaking whenever a new bank formats things slightly differently. we need one unified thing that handles all the weird formats banks use — some banks put everything on one line, some leave tags unclosed, etc.

the library should expose account info, transactions with proper types and amounts, and also the sign-on/institution block. amounts need to be normalized properly regardless of whether the bank uses commas or dots as separators. dates too — banks include varying levels of precision and sometimes timezone stuff that should NOT change the actual displayed time.

should be structured like we did with the payment processor adapter — separate concerns, not one giant file. the error handling should be clean categories, not raw exceptions bubbling up.

also need a test runner script that loops through case files and dumps outputs for comparison. fixture files will be in a known location. check how the login module handled the dialect normalization step for reference on how to structure that part.

one thing to clarify on amounts: for transaction amounts, purely integral values should lose the trailing decimal zeros, so 200.00 should come out as 200, -100.00 as -100, and 150.00 as 150. but that cleanup is only for transaction amounts. ledger balances should not get that treatment, so values like -500.00 and 5250.00 stay exactly as -500.00 and 5250.00.

also on amount coercion, we do need to treat both period and comma as possible decimal marks and also handle thousands separators. so 1000.00, 1000,00, 1,000.00, and 1.000,00 should all normalize to 1000. there’s also that documented quirk where a bare integer of three or more digits whose last two digits look like a fractional part is treated as having an implied two-decimal fraction, so bare '100' is interpreted as 1 because the trailing 00 gets consumed as cents. and again, purely integral results drop trailing zeros.

for timestamps, the bracketed timezone offset suffix like [-5:EST] and any fractional seconds are not supposed to shift the wall-clock time at all. we treat the timestamp as a wall-clock value and render it as-is in Y-m-d H:i:s format. so 20081005132200.124[-5:EST] should render as 2008-10-05 13:22:00, with no UTC adjustment.

on transaction typing, every transaction object should include both the raw type code and the derived human-readable description. the mappings we need are CREDIT → 'Generic credit', CHECK → 'Cheque', and POS → 'Point of sale debit or credit'.

one more edge case on dates: if the source leaves out DTUSER, then userInitiatedDate should be the literal string 'none'. not an empty string, not null, and not copied from the posting date. only if DTUSER actually exists in the source should userInitiatedDate render as a wall-clock datetime.

for errors, we only want two clean categories in output: 'parse_failed' when the content can’t be normalized into a valid document, and 'file_not_found' when the path doesn’t exist. those should render exactly as 'error=parse_failed' and 'error=file_not_found'. nothing else should leak through there — no raw exceptions, stack traces, or warning text.

and just to make the structure expectation explicit, the dialect normalization step from the PRD should be its own dedicated pre-processing unit that converts SGML unclosed-tag format, single-line format, and whitespace variants into well-formed markup before the structured-document reader runs. that should live as a separate module/file, not be inlined into the parser.

trigger_keywords: how does the single account helper flag work
when is singleAccountHelper set vs unset
what controls the singleAccountHelper value
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The singleAccountHelper field is 'set' when the document contains exactly one account, and 'unset' when it contains more than one account. It must appear in both 'accounts' and 'summary' operation outputs.

trigger_keywords: how to handle credit card account blocks
what fields are empty for card statements
credit card vs bank account structure differences
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Credit card statements nest account identity under a card-account block (CCACCTFROM in OFX) rather than a bank-account block (BANKACCTFROM). When the parser detects a card-account source, the card number becomes the accountNumber, and both accountType and routingNumber render as empty string. The output structure is otherwise identical to a bank statement.

trigger_keywords: how should the test runner script work
what does the test harness output
where does the test script write results
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test runner is invoked as 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' and accepts an optional '--cases-dir <subdir>' argument (defaulting to 'test_cases'). For each case in each *.json file it feeds the input to the adapter and writes raw stdout to rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{stem}@{idx:03d}.txt — one file per case, zero-padded three-digit index, containing only program output for direct comparison against expected_output.

trigger_keywords: how to handle the SGML unclosed tag dialect
normalizing legacy OFX with missing closing tags
multi-line format with unclosed value tags
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dialect normalization stage must handle legacy SGML-style OFX where value tags are unclosed (e.g. <ACCTID>12345 with no </ACCTID>). It must also handle: fully single-line documents, indentation/whitespace variations, and trailing marketing or info text after the document body. After normalization all dialects produce the same structured parse result.

trigger_keywords: how is the summary operation different from accounts
what fields does the summary op return
currencies field format in summary output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The 'summary' operation returns: accountCount, singleAccountHelper (set/unset), transactionCount (total across all accounts), and currencies (sorted distinct currency codes as a comma-separated or single value). The 'accounts' operation returns full account and transaction detail. The 'signon' operation returns statusCode, statusSeverity, statusMessage, statusCodeDesc, language, instituteName, instituteId, and date.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

are there public test cases

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'accounts', 'signon', 'summary', 'amount', 'datetime', 'parse', 'load_path'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: structured like we did with the payment processor adapter — separate concerns, not one giant file
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The multi-file architecture requirement: dialect normalization, structured-document reader, value coercion helpers (amount/date), and typed entity model must be in distinct units with a clear directory tree. The JSON stdin/stdout adapter must be a thin separate layer with no knowledge of I/O leaking into the core parser.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect. Please check the existing fixture files in the rcb_tests directory and the expected_output fields in the test case JSON files for the precise contract — those are the ground truth for how outputs should be formatted.

feature2_signon.json.description: From the sign-on block, report response status (code, severity, message, and the description the code maps to), session language, institution name/id, and the server timestamp as wall-clock time. Code 0 maps to 'Success'; an absent message is empty.

cases.expected_output:
statusCode=0
statusSeverity=INFO
statusMessage=
statusCodeDesc=Success
language=ENG
instituteName=MYBANK
instituteId=01234
date=2007-10-15 02:15:29

feature3_multiple_accounts.json.description: Summarize a document by total account count, total transaction count across accounts, and sorted distinct currencies. With more than one account the single-account helper is unset; with one account it is set.

feature4_credit_card.json.description: Parse a credit-card statement whose account identity lives in a card-account source block, producing the same structure as a bank statement: the card number as account id, empty routing/type, preserved signed balance and amount.

accountCount=1
singleAccountHelper=set

account[0].accountType=
account[0].routingNumber=
account[0].agencyNumber=
account[0].transactionUid=20160823000000
account[0].balance=-500.00

feature5_dialect_tolerance.json.description: Load statement documents in several serialization dialects (multi-line with unclosed value tags, fully single-line, whitespace variants, trailing info text) and confirm each normalizes to the same structured result, reported as account count, single-account helper flag, total transaction count, and sorted distinct currencies.

feature6_amount_normalization.json.description: Coerce a money string in any locale convention (period/comma decimal mark, optional thousands separators, optional leading minus) into one numeric value; integral results drop trailing zeros.

feature7_date_parsing.json.description: Parse a timestamp at any supported precision (date only, date-time, date-time with fractional seconds, date-time with fractional seconds plus bracketed timezone offset) into a wall-clock 'Y-m-d H:i:s' value; the offset and fractional components do not shift the wall-clock fields.

feature8_error_handling.json.description: Surface failures as neutral categories: content that cannot be normalized into a valid document yields a parse-failure category; a path that does not exist yields a file-not-found category.
